data.title: PDD Q4 Earnings Miss: I Warned You About The End Of Their Hypergrowth
data.description:
Introduction

Back in November, I wrote an article and warned that the era of hyper-growth for PDD Holdings Inc. (PDD) was coming to an end, and unfortunately for the shareholders, the market thereafter decided to agree with me, sending the stock down. At the time, I was skeptical regarding Temu’s sustainability, noting how its aggressive tactics reminded me far too much of the early days of Wish, which did not end well, even though both the results and the stock were surging at some point in 2020. Besides, I said that even a cheap valuation multiple couldn't protect investors from the massive geopolitical risks that weigh on all of the Chinese stocks.

Since that analysis, the stock is down by about 20%, as both of my arguments proved to be correct. PDD stock is down together with all of its Chinese peers, and the recent Q4 earnings show the company isn’t doing as well as everyone hoped it would.

PDD missed on both revenue and earnings expectations, with quarterly revenue of RMB123.91 billion and an adjusted EPS of RMB17.69 coming in below what analysts expected to see. While the company did manage a 12% revenue increase for the quarter, the broader trend doesn’t look great, and management decided that it was time to make heavy supply chain investments that they admit will "inevitably affect" financial performance. In this article, I’ll break down this quarter and try to answer the question of whether it is a compelling buying opportunity now, after the stock went down by 20%.

“The external environment and competitive landscape are undergoing rapid changes. To meet the evolving needs of consumers, we must continually explore and make investments,” said Ms. Jun Liu, VP of Finance of PDD Holdings. “These investments are firm and long-term, and will inevitably affect our financial performance.” - Earnings Release.

Q4 2025

As I already mentioned, total revenues were of RMB123,912.2 million, which was a 12% increase year-over-year. While 12% it’s not a weak growth, and actually, it was better than any other quarter of 2025, the company has still missed the target of RMB124.71 billion that analysts predicted they’d see. Anyway, the days of explosive growth with 60% to 120% growth year-over-year are long gone, and the company isn’t coming back there.

There are two areas from which the money comes - online marketing services, which brought in RMB60,010.1 million, and transaction services, with RMB63,902.1 million, which was an increase of 19%. Once again, even though the numbers are not straightforwardly low, it is very far away from how the company was going before, throughout 2024.

Net income did not do as well as revenue (and yes, even though the revenue had disappointed). It came in at RMB24,541.0 million, which is an 11% drop from the last quarter of 2024, which is a concerning sign. Non-GAAP diluted earnings per ADS also did not reach the estimate of RMB20.71. It means business operations cost more, but the revenues are not rising by the same measure. The reasons are various, starting from the competition to the lower consumer spending, and it weighs on margins. By the way, not only is PDD struggling, but retail is weak for its competitors as well.

Breaking down what happened exactly, we would see that the total costs of revenues increased 15% to RMB55,155.6 million, largely driven by higher fulfillment fees and server costs. This is a major confirmation of my thesis regarding Temu, as fulfillment fees are the logistics of getting a package delivered to the customer. PDD takes these costs on itself to keep people ordering, and its heavy expansion into the international markets contributes to this massive burden.

Once again, it’s like Wish all over again. I remember those days so well, as I had a YouTube channel then, where a host talked mostly about Wish breaking down the business model and the stock price. There was a market frenzy over it, as during the pandemic, all of the physical retailers were shutting down, and digital shopping was everything. The stock was unstoppable until the time came to monetize those acquired users with shipping and higher prices. The moment that happened, everyone returned to order from Aliexpress. On the digital side, bandwidth and server costs also add up as apps and websites run for millions of simultaneous users globally.

Operating expenses are up 10%, and sales and marketing are a huge expense for obvious reasons. The competition is enormous, so this is a necessary investment to keep selling. What was indeed unexpected was the 32% surge in research and development spending, meaning the company tries to find other ways besides sales to attract new people, but it doesn’t seem to be translating into revenue, at least for now.

At the same time, for the full year of 2025, the situation isn’t horrible. The company performed, even if relatively slowly, with RMB431,845.7 million in total revenue, which was a 10% increase from 2024. Full-year operating profit decreased by 13% to RMB94,624.1 million. The company is having to spend significantly more just to maintain its current position, without even reaccelerating in growth. Net cash generated from operating activities went down to RMB106,938.7 million compared to over RMB121 billion the year before. As I already mentioned, it looks like the situation will only worsen, and the management acknowledges it, making their biggest bet on supply chain investment, which is a huge shift for this company, as in the past, PDD used to be a company with a capital-light business model, connecting buyers and sellers. By investing in the supply chain, they are beginning to build warehouses, logistics, and everything else needed to survive the changing competitive landscape.

Whether it will be a successful shift, only time will tell, but it is definitely a very risky decision. Alibaba’s (BABA) infrastructure isn’t helping its retail segment to grow, so I’m not sure how PDD will be different.

Despite the miss and the risky future, PDD has a lot of cash, and it is a huge plus in these uncertain times. As of December 31, 2025, their cash, cash equivalents, and short-term investments totaled RMB422.3 billion, up from RMB331.6 billion a year earlier. This gives them a huge safety net, but for me, it raises the question of whether this business model is not too expensive to keep.

Valuation

Looking at the valuation, PDD is cheap, but there is a reason for this. It is way cheaper than the sector, and even more at a discount compared to its own 5-year average, but the growth has slowed significantly as well.

Even though the company is still incredibly profitable and has a huge pile of cash (RMB422.3 billion to be exact, which is about $60 billion), the risk profile has changed. Management itself is warning that the high-margin days are over, and now the supply chain will be a heavy investment they have to make.

Conclusion

To conclude, it is clear that PDD is still not in a bad position; it is not shrinking, but the results I am seeing today are another confirmation that the easy growth is gone, and it is not coming back. The management is being very transparent about the fact that building out the supply chain will drag on profits for the foreseeable future. There are no promises when we will see results from this investment. For me, the risks I saw back in November (from the Temu and the geopolitical environment) are still here, while another risk of pivoting away from an asset-light business model adds additional uncertainty.

Interestingly, the stock is actually up as I am writing this, surging about 2% in pre-market trading despite the earnings miss. It is most likely due to the low valuation multiple, mixed with hopes for an agreement between the U.S. and Iran, and also the company’s massive RMB422.3 billion cash position, which smooths the worries of the market. However, I am not a fan of this specific setup, and also not a fan of investing in Chinese companies while geopolitical tensions are so pronounced. I am rating PDD a Hold, but I’d not consider a position myself for now.

data.title: Surge Energy: Benefiting From Higher Oil Prices
data.description:
Introduction

Surge Energy (SGY:CA) (ZPTAF) is a Canadian oil and gas producer with a very decent output of approximately 23,000 barrels of oil-equivalent per day. With an average WTI oil price below US$60/barrel on a WTI basis in the fourth quarter of 2025, the expectations weren’t very high. But given where the oil price is trading at right now, the first quarter could and should be pretty strong despite existing hedge positions.

The Q4 2025 results: Somewhat irrelevant given the current oil price environment

In the final quarter of 2025, Surge’s production came in at just under 23,200 barrels of oil-equivalent per day. Fortunately for the company, the majority of the oil-equivalent production actually consisted of oil and natural gas liquids, and the ratio of natural gas in the production mix remains quite low at approximately 12%. As the natural gas price remains low (C$2.23/mcf in Q4 2025, with an average realized price of C$1.75), the contribution of the natural gas sales to the total revenue is negligible, at approximately 2%.

The company’s total revenue for the entire financial year came in at just under C$572 million, and this is based on an average production rate of roughly 23,500 barrels of oil-equivalent per day, and an average WTI price of just under US$65/barrel.

As the income statement below shows, the net revenue came in at almost exactly C$500 million after taking the royalty payments and some small hedging gains into account.

The operating expenses and the transportation expenses decreased, and this helped Surge Energy post a very robust result. The bottom line indicates a net income of just over C$40 million for an EPS of C$0.41. This includes a non-cash impairment charge to the tune of C$18.4 million, which impacted the pre-tax result by about 19 cents per share.

As Surge is a dividend-paying company, I’m of course very interested in the company’s cash flow profile, especially at $65 oil, to ensure the dividends remain well-covered. As you can see below, the total operating cash flow came in at C$266 million, mainly because the company added back the impairment expenses as well as the tax expense, as Surge won’t have to pay cash taxes in the foreseeable future as it is working through its historical losses.

We should also deduct the C$5.2 million in lease payments and end up with an adjusted operating cash flow of approximately C$260 million.

We see the total capex was approximately C$160 million, which means the net underlying free cash flow was almost exactly C$100 million, for an FCFPS of C$1.02. Keep in mind this is based on $65 oil and approximately C$2-3/barrel in realized hedging gains.

This means that A) the dividends are well covered (Surge Energy spent just under C$52 million on dividends – the current dividend of C$0.0433 per month represents a dividend yield of 6.1%), while the C$8.7 million in share buybacks were also fully covered by the incoming cash flow. Surge Energy ended the year with almost C$19 million in cash on its balance sheet, while it had just under C$175 million in gross debt. That’s manageable at $65 oil, and that’s very manageable at $90 oil, so I am hoping to see a seasonality-adjusted net debt decrease in Q1 2026.

What can we expect in 2026?

Surge Energy published its expectations for 2026 in the final quarter of last year, and its budget was based on an average WTI oil price of approximately $65/barrel.

The company expects to produce an average of 23,000 barrels of oil-equivalent per day (with the same 88/12 ratio of liquids to natural gas), and it expects to record a funds flow of C$265 million.

The total capex to keep the production flat is estimated at approximately C$150 million, resulting in a net free cash flow of C$95M after also taking some other expenses into consideration, for approximately C$0.98 per share.

While the current oil price is a blessing, keep in mind that Surge Energy has entered into hedging contracts, and approximately a quarter of the oil in Q1 has been locked in at US$66/barrel on a WTI basis, while approximately 45% of the Q2 volumes have been hedged as well. This means that the Q1 results may be a bit weaker than anticipated, while the company may actually post a net loss as it will have to record an unrealized hedging loss on the open positions.

If the oil price on the market averages $85/barrel this year, I estimate Surge’s average realized oil price (on a WTI basis) will be approximately $76/barrel. Using that in the model for 2026 should add approximately C$80 million to the net free cash flow, boosting the FCFPS to approximately C$1.75.

Investment thesis

It will be interesting to see if Surge Energy has layered in additional hedges in the first quarter of this year. If the company was happy to hedge at $65-$66 oil, I can only hope it adds some additional protection at strip prices, just to lock in the incremental revenue. I also hope and expect to see more hedges for H1 2027, where – as of the end of December – only 8% of the anticipated oil production is subject to swaps at $67-$68. With oil futures for delivery in H1 2027 at $72-$76/barrel, I wouldn’t mind seeing some additional hedges despite the price on the futures market being lower than the current spot price.

I have a long position in Surge Energy, and I have a long position in its convertible debentures (which are listed on the TSX). Although 25% of its oil production in Q1 and 45% of its Q2 production has been hedged, the company should benefit from the current oil price environment.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Virtus Ceredex Large-Cap Value Equity Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Review

Value stocks led U.S. markets higher in the fourth quarter, with large-cap value broadly outperforming growth counterparts. Artificial intelligence (AI) remained a dominant performance driver. Yet, significant multiple expansion across the AI trade helped to prompt emerging signs of market broadening, as investors diversified into a wider range of more attractively priced companies delivering above-expectations earnings growth. Cyclically sensitive stocks, however, largely continued to lag. Federal Reserve rate cuts and resilient U.S. economic growth provided a supportive backdrop, encouraging investors to look ahead to broadening fundamental strength across a wider set of end markets.

Performance

The Virtus Ceredex Large-Cap Value Equity Fund returned +2.84% (Class I)(STVTX) for the quarter compared to the Russell 1000® Value Index's return of +3.81%. Industrials, financials, and energy stock selection were the largest sector contributors to performance. Consumer discretionary, materials, and consumer staples stock selection were the largest sector detractors from performance.

Alphabet and Western Digital were among the strongest stock contributors.

  • Leading technology conglomerate Alphabet (GOOG) outperformed following stronger-than-expected financial results driven by accelerated Google Cloud growth and impressive AI innovation and integration across its business lines. We initiated the position earlier in 2025 amid antitrust concerns that prompted a sharp selloff in the stock, when the intrinsic worth of its parts appeared fundamentally undervalued. We held steady with the position as it continued to rise during the quarter, seeing room for continued upside.
  • Data storage solutions company Western Digital (WDC) continued to outperform on strong demand for its high-capacity hard disk drives (HDDs). The surge is being driven by AI companies deploying reinforcement learning models, which require retaining massive datasets, and by a concurrent shortage and price spike in NAND memory, prompting businesses to turn to HDDs as a cost-effective alternative. We bought the stock when memory prices were falling amid inventory destocking and have realized impressive gains on the AI catalyst, trimming the position while continuing to hold on strong fundamentals.

Eaton and Lennar were among the largest stock detractors.

  • We sold power management and electrical solutions company Eaton (ETN), following an acquisition related to data center cooling that we expect the technology for AI will be moving away from. We believe this could limit the company's growth prospects, prompting us to reallocate capital to higher-conviction opportunities.
  • Home construction company Lennar (LEN) underperformed due to a continued weak housing environment. While we trimmed the position around price action in the quarter, we believe the stock is hitting a cyclical trough that is being overly discounted by investors. Its efforts to lower construction costs for first-time homebuyers, in particular, position it to capture pent-up demand as the housing cycle improves, which we expect may lead to significant earnings growth.

Portfolio Changes

New positions included Target and Teradyne. Exits included Walmart, 3M, and Johnson & Johnson.

  • We sold Walmart (WMT) and swapped into Target (TGT), which we believe offers a stronger retailer turnaround story under its new CEO, who is heavily investing in pricing strategies and store upgrades.
  • We bought backend semiconductor testing company Teradyne (TER), which we believe continues to offer underestimated earnings potential relative to current valuations, even at this point in the AI cycle.
  • We sold industrials conglomerate 3M (MMM) and healthcare conglomerate Johnson & Johnson (JNJ) after they reached their price targets, both multiyear turnaround stories that paid off handsomely.

Outlook

We believe the Fund enters 2026 well positioned and fundamentally strong. Within AI-driven stocks, upside potential remains meaningful, but opportunities are evolving alongside notable earnings, margin, and capex expansion, prompting a broader positioning for mid-stage market growth.

Across other holdings, we maintain a procyclical tilt, which has moderated near-term performance. While we may have been early in this analysis, the current backdrop for cyclical recovery appears increasingly compelling. Inventory levels have largely been right-sized, and pent-up demand, rising commercial lending, tariff-driven supply chain adjustments, falling interest rates, and new tax incentives all suggest a likely acceleration in broader capex spending, supporting substantial earnings growth potential in many currently undervalued cyclical names. Further, initial gains in the early stages of cyclical recoveries historically tend to come from more speculative, higher-beta stocks, as was the case in value markets for much of 2025. That outperformance has now considerably narrowed as market leadership has begun to broaden, suggesting that fundamentals may be starting to align for wider end-market expansion.

data.title: Lithium Argentina 2025 Results Disappointing, But Still Remains On My Bargain-Hunting List
data.description:
Investment thesis.

Lithium Argentina's (LAR) latest results were a bit of a disappointment, missing expectations. The markets greeted the results mostly positively, despite the large miss on earnings. A long-term positive outlook for lithium demand, coupled with the company's growth prospects make it an enticing investment opportunity that I continue to keep on my bargain-hunting list of stocks, in expectation of what will eventually be some deeper and broader stock market selloffs in response to the Iran war's continuation.

Maintaining a hold position.

The last time I covered Lithium Argentina was back in November, when I had it as a hold. At that point, I was coming to terms with the reality that my lithium market strategy and related investments were perhaps my most significant mistake in 2025, which was otherwise a rather successful year for me. I was expecting to see one more significant pullback in lithium market prices, and my biggest mistake by far was that I decided to liquidate most of my lithium miners from my portfolio last year, with the intent to buy back shares on an expected sizable pullback, which never materialized.

The company's share price declined recently from its all-time high of above $8.50. I already had a modest position in Lithium Argentina stock when I bought some more shares when it dipped under $6/share this month. It has since recovered to around $7/share, so I am back to maintaining a hold position. With the Iran war's effect on the markets, my current rating might not last long, since market conditions are currently volatile.

Lithium Argentina's 2025 financial results at a glance.

For the year 2025, Lithium Argentina closed off the year with a loss of 47 cents/share instead of an expectation of a positive net income of 3 cents/share. It was a loss of $76.88 million for the year, versus a loss of just over $15 million in 2024.

As we can see, the net loss widened compared with 2024. A significant factor was the sizable increase in financing costs that reached almost $32 million, versus $25 million in the prior year. It should be noted that this increase in finance costs is not coming with an equally impressive increase in output in 2026. In other words, Lithium Argentina is taking on significant financial burdens to expand output; however, the benefits in terms of production growth will only be fully reflected in its financial results further down the line.

Lithium Argentina is set to see a significant output increase this year from its main producing site, as the chart above shows. Higher output, combined with potentially higher lithium market prices this year, should help to improve its financial results. Still, we are far from seeing some solid and consistent earnings from this company.

Lithium market outlook.

The global EV market is off to a rough start.

Sales were down Y-O-Y for the first two months of 2026. February sales are particularly worrying, since the decline in sales has reached double digits. While it is still early going, one has to wonder whether we will see any global EV sales growth this year.

The spike in oil prices this month, due to the war in Iran, is arguably a positive factor for global EV sales prospects, because higher oil prices tend to make the economics of buying & operating an EV more appealing, as the cost of driving conventional cars goes up. At the same time, a prolonged disruption of oil & gas exports from the ME region has the potential to cause significant damage to the global economy. As I pointed out in a recent article, a 12-week duration of the Iran war can potentially shave off about 3% from global GDP this decade. A significant and sustained slowdown in economic growth has the potential to impact consumer spending more broadly, which could impact EV sales.

Looking beyond the potential effects of the Iran war, I see a global oil market that may be tighter than the likes of the EIA & IEA have been prophesying in the past few years. As I explained in a recent article, there seem to be increasingly obvious discrepancies between the market balance being presented by the likes of the EIA and its own data. If I am correct, oil prices will be sustainably higher for the long term.

A sustained, but more gentle rise in oil prices is less likely to have the potential negative impact on the economy that a sudden price spike that we might see this year, due to the disruption of ME oil & gas flows, might cause. At the same time, a steady and prolonged rise in oil prices is likely to gently shift consumer preferences more toward buying electric personal vehicles. In other words, the long-term bullish EV sales growth outlook that many institutions are forecasting may still be intact, even if we experience a global decline in EV sales this year.

Perhaps it will not be as dramatic an increase in EV sales as it is illustrated in the chart above, but still a robust increase, which in turn will lead to continued steady demand growth for lithium.

Investment implications.

Most of the roughly 25% decline in Lithium America's share price from the all-time high, to the point when I bought this month, occurred since the start of the Iran war. If one assumes most of the effects of the war to be transitory, that alone suggests that Lithium Argentina stock is a bargain at current levels. The company's poor financial performance suggests it may still be a while before it becomes profitable; therefore, it could be argued that the market got ahead of itself on this stock when it reached a new all-time high. Furthermore, the global EV boom is slowing, perhaps temporarily, perhaps not, which could push lithium prices down in the short term, especially if a broad global economic slowdown is triggered by the deepening effects of the ongoing Iran war.

At the same time, a bet on the EV boom continuing is a bet on the global economy continuing to expand, including continued growth in personal transport vehicles. The fact remains that even with EV sales in the 20 million/year range, global oil demand is still rising, with no obvious sources of continued growth left on the planet to make up for demand growth, as well as all the producing regions that are seeing a decline in oil output. In other words, we need to see continued growth in EV sales to prevent a further slowdown in global GDP growth. Within this context, I believe that lithium demand growth prospects are solid, as long as there is a will to keep the global economy on track.

As far as comparing Lithium Argentina to industry peers, as I pointed out in a recent article, I am also looking at a good entry point to start buying SQM (SQM) shares.

As we can see, Lithium Argentina's stock price has been outperforming its peers. At the same time, some metrics make the past performance stand out as arguably not supported by the fundamentals. For instance, Albemarle (ALB) has a forward P/E ratio of over 21, SQM is at over 15, while Lithium Argentina is at over 17, in the middle of the pack. All three companies are geared toward higher output; it is therefore not a matter of growth expectations. One thing we can expect is for the likes of Albemarle & SQM, which are well-established companies, to provide more steady, dependable financial results, with fewer major surprises, as we saw with Lithium Argentina with its latest results.

Ideally, I want to add Albemarle and SQM shares to my portfolio as well, in addition to Lithium Argentina, which is my main lithium mining position currently. I also have a very small position in Lithium Americas (LAC) stocks. I expect the lithium market to remain volatile for the foreseeable future, meaning that, in addition to seeking advantageous entry points for lower downside risk and higher upside potential, I want to keep my lithium exposure diversified. After taking a first initial nibble at Lithium Argentina shares on Iran war-related stock movements, I intend to hold off and wait for a more advantageous position before I commit to adding more, with an inclination to start adding SQM & Albemarle shares, before adding more Lithium Argentina shares to my portfolio.

Risks to my thesis.

Given that I am currently in a holding pattern, waiting for a better entry point, there is a risk that I may miss out on what may end up becoming a sustained lithium price rally in the coming years. The current war in Iran may be the best opportunity to buy lithium miners.

Lithium prices arguably already entered a sustained bull market last year, as the chart shows. The latest global EV sales results suggest that the bottoming in the lithium market may have been a bit premature. There is a risk that the Iran war will end abruptly, with markets rallying across the board, pulling lithium miners along, regardless of lithium market fundamentals, leaving me on the sidelines, with only a very small exposure to the lithium market.

Conclusions.

The market mostly greeted Lithium Argentina's results positively, citing production cost reductions as some of the positive takeaways.

While I acknowledge these factors, I also opted to focus on other aspects that I see taking precedent in the shorter term, such as the external market conditions and the company's growing debt-servicing burden that may turn out to be a particularly worrying factor within the context of growing global upheaval. I will maintain a hold position for now, even if it means risking missing out.

data.title: U.S. Budget Deficit Pressures Mount As War Spending Surges
data.description:
By James Picerno

The US budget deficit was already on shaky ground before the Iran war, and reports that the Pentagon will seek an additional $200 billion in military funding only complicate the outlook for fiscal risk. Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth has also suggested the request could rise depending on operational needs.

Whatever the extra costs associated with the ongoing conflict, higher spending arrives at a difficult time for the US budget outlook. In February, the Congressional Budget Office (CBO) revised its forecast for government finances and projected that the already hefty deficit will deepen this year to $1.853 trillion. Over the course of the decade ahead, the non-partisan CBO estimates that the deficit-to-GDP ratio will increase to 6.7% from the current 5.7%. If correct, the rise would mark the highest ratio since the pandemic.

The prospect of bigger deficits may be worrisome for several reasons, starting with the fact that the current level of debt is unusual for an economy that’s not in recession. Atypical or not, the fiscal burden poses a potential threat to financial markets by raising inflation and interest rates.

TMC Research routinely analyzes the data in this corner, but fiscal risk modeling remains inherently imprecise for estimating the true level of vulnerability and gauging debt markets’ tolerance for government indebtedness. But this much is clear: if the current level of outstanding government obligations increases from current levels - especially in relation to the size of the economy - the implied risk also increases.

“Higher debt adds to the risk of inflationary pressure in both the short- and the long-run, through aggregate demand, inflation expectations, crowding-out of private investment, and worries about fiscal dominance,” advises The Budget Lab at Yale.

Fiscal Risk Index

US fiscal pressure has increased lately, according to TMC Research’s Fiscal Risk Index (FRI). The current reading rose to 0.4, rebounding once again to the highest level since the pandemic.

The increase shows that four of FRI’s ten inputs (see table below) are flashing warning signs, relatively speaking, based on our assumptions for modeling the data.

For another perspective, the Fiscal Risk: Daily Bias Indicator sums the z-scores for the ten inputs above, providing a more sensitive profile of how the aggregate mix is trending. On that basis, fiscal risk is approaching levels last seen during the pandemic (see chart below).

Our standard caveat for these indicators is worth repeating - namely, modeling fiscal risk is challenging for several reasons, and the analysis above is, at best, a rough first approximation. In addition to the difficulty of quantitatively estimating the level of fiscal risk, there’s also debate about how much debt the financial markets will bear before demanding a significantly higher risk premium.

Further complicating the picture is a recent research paper arguing that financialization and deficit-fueled spending have been key drivers of profit growth and market expansion, propping up elevated equity valuations even as fiscal vulnerabilities mount.

The common theme from our analysis that aligns with reporting from other sources: the deficit trend is still moving in the wrong direction. That’s worrisome on its face, and even more problematic when you consider that Washington has yet to lay out acredible plan to address the rising tide of red ink.

Original Source: The Milwaukee Company

data.title: Arm Holdings: A Bold Move Into AI Chips And I'm Bullish
data.description:
Thesis

Arm Holdings plc (ARM) is a semiconductor design company based in the UK. It creates the designs for processors used in billions of devices, from smartphones and cloud infrastructure to increasingly AI systems.

Arm shares are moving higher in pre-market trading after the company introduced its AGI CPU for AI data centers and laid out ambitious long-term expectations for the product. The announcement, along with partnerships with companies such as Meta Platforms (META) and OpenAI, and manufacturing handled by Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company (TSM), signals a real strategic pivot for the company.

Instead of only licensing its chip designs to other companies, Arm is starting to sell its own CPUs designed for data centers.

After going over the news, factoring in the data, and reflecting on the implications, I’m leaning bullish because the goal is to capture more of the value from AI infrastructure rather than remain a supplier of underlying designs.

Arm Holdings: Expanding Beyond Chip Design

Arm’s business model has historically been simple. The company doesn't manufacture chips itself. Instead, it designs the processor blueprints that other companies use to build their own chips. Companies such as Apple (AAPL), Qualcomm (QCOM), Nvidia (NVDA), and many smartphone chip manufacturers license Arm’s designs and then incorporate those designs into the processors they produce. But AI infrastructure changes the economics of chips.

  • CSS share of Arm royalties: ~20%
  • Years since CSS introduction: ~3–4 years.

AI chips, especially the ones used in cloud data centers, are incredibly complex systems. It’s a whole system-on-chip architecture problem. And that’s where CSS (Compute Subsystems) come in. Instead of selling just a CPU core, Arm sells something closer to a pre-integrated platform: CPU clusters, interconnects, memory systems, and other components already designed to work together. So CSS reduces the time and engineering work companies need to build a chip.

  • CPU cores per gigawatt (pre-agentic AI): ~30 million
  • CPU cores per gigawatt (agentic AI estimate): ~120 million
  • Increase in tokens per human with agents: ~15×.

Moving into compute subsystems has already helped the company make more money. This is because more of the design work is packaged together at the beginning, and customers spend less time integrating the technology into their own systems. Arm also said that cloud AI royalty revenue was still growing quickly, while adoption of CSS continued to expand among customers building edge and mobile devices. The takeaway is that the old model isn't broken. Essentially, the new chip is an attempt to capture a much larger share of system value.

As AI systems become more agent-based and focused on coordinating many tasks, the CPU takes on more responsibility for managing and organizing the work. During a recent shareholder call, CEO Haas and Executive VP of Cloud AI Business Unit Mohamed Awad repeatedly explain that demand's being driven more by things like task scheduling, coordination between different parts of the system, memory access, and overall system throughput. And they argue that efficiently managing and moving work around the system is becoming just as important as the speed of the accelerators themselves.

Recent reports also support the bigger industry trend. As AI inference keeps growing, power limits in data centers become tighter, and AI workflows become more complex, data centers are putting more emphasis on efficient host computing. Both Reuters and Arm’s launch materials suggest that agentic AI creates an opportunity for Arm to sell power-efficient CPUs in a market that has traditionally been dominated by existing suppliers. However, I wouldn't automatically jump the gun and assume that this opportunity will easily turn into revenue for Arm. Even if CPUs become a bottleneck, that does not mean Arm will automatically win the business. Large cloud companies (the hyperscalers) already have their own internal chip development plans and long-standing relationships with current chip suppliers, which could make it harder for Arm to muscle into that market.

Now, from a financial perspective, the key change is that Arm's moving closer to earning product-based revenue. That'll bring both advantages and challenges. In contrast, Arm’s traditional license and royalty model has allowed the business to grow without taking on much balance-sheet risk, requiring less working capital, and generating high additional profit margins once Arm’s chip designs are built into products.

  • AGI CPU cores per chip: up to 136 V3 cores
  • CPU frequency: up to 3.7 GHz
  • Memory bandwidth: ~6 GB/s per core
  • Memory latency: <100 ns
  • TDP: ~300 W
  • Process node: 3 nm (TSMC).

Basically, selling an actual physical chip could significantly increase how much revenue Arm earns from each deployment. Even so, it drags in operational challenges the old licensing model mostly sidestepped. These include coordinating manufacturing, handling chip validation and qualification, providing platform support, and managing customer-specific product roadmaps. It could also make gross margins less predictable. Profitability would depend on factors such as how quickly production ramps up, manufacturing yield (how many usable chips come from each batch), and, of course, pricing discipline.

Management tried to ease those concerns by saying the chip is built on the same Neoverse technology that's already widely used across the industry. Because many companies already work with Neoverse, this should make the software easier to run on the new chip and lower the risk of companies having trouble adopting it. That explanation helps somewhat, but it doesn’t completely solve the problems. There could still be conflicts with Arm’s existing partners in the sales channel, and support costs could increase.

The list of customers involved makes the launch seem more believable. Meta’s involvement is especially important, because it suggests that Arm didn’t create the chip just hoping someone would need it. In the discussion transcript, the company keeps emphasizing that customers were asking for this product, rather than it being something Arm decided to build on its own. Reports from outside sources also support this, saying that Meta was the main design partner and is expected to be the first company to deploy the chip at scale.

  • Projected annual revenue from AGI CPU by 2031: ~$15B
  • Projected total Arm annual revenue by 2031: ~$25B
  • Projected EPS by 2031: ~$9
  • Current AI data center royalty TAM: ~$3B
  • Future CPU TAM opportunity: ~$100B
  • Potential total TAM by end of decade: >$1T.

Personally, I see that as the strongest positive sign. Synopsys’ (SNPS) involvement also adds more value. Alongside this, Meta is the main deployment partner that plans to use the chip, and TSMC will handle the manufacturing. Because these are well-known and experienced partners, it reduces some of the risks around testing, validation, and readiness. However, when a first-generation product is heavily designed around the needs of one hyperscaler, it can make short-term demand look very strong, but that doesn’t necessarily mean the product will have wide demand across the broader market in the long run.

There is also a strategic conflict here. Arm says the market is big enough for everyone, but by making and selling its own chips, it is moving closer to competing with the same companies that license its technology, including firms that have much larger systems and platform ecosystems. Both Barron’s and Reuters pointed out this risk, and it’s something investors should pay close attention to.

ARM's Valuation

At the time of my analysis, ARM has ~1.062 billion shares outstanding with a market capitalization of $143 billion, that is, prior to today's pre-market trading. This works out to a stock trading at about 77x forward non-GAAP P/E, about 29x sales, and about 60x EBITDA.

On these metrics, let's call them stale, the stock appears very expensive when compared to its current financial performance. This view's also consistent with the FAST Graphs fair-value model. Using a more typical 21x earnings multiple, FAST Graphs estimates the stock’s fair value to be closer to, well, ridiculously lower, ~ $43 per share.

However, if management reaches its $25 billion revenue target by 2031, with about $15 billion expected to come from the new AGI CPU product line, the valuation would look very different. If the market capitalization stayed the same as today, the company’s price-to-sales ratio would fall to about 5.7x revenue, instead of the much higher level it is trading at today.

And that would put the company’s valuation in a much more reasonable range for a high-margin semiconductor company that's already built and scaled a large ecosystem.

The earnings outlook removes some of the ambiguity. If the company earns $9 per share with 1.062 billion shares outstanding, that would equal about $9.6 billion in total net income. At today’s market capitalization of about $143 billion, that level of profit would mean the stock is trading at roughly 15x earnings.

That would represent a huge drop from the current valuation. And it helps explain why the stock can trade at such a high multiple today without necessarily being irrational. That improvement in valuation only happens if the company actually reaches those targets without margins shrinking along the way. To earn $9 per share on $25 billion in revenue, the company would need net profit margins of about 38%.

Final Takeaway

Buy. But it's not risk-free or simple. If Arm successfully delivers on its AGI CPU roadmap, today’s high valuation could come down to a much more reasonable level as the business grows into it. Naturally if Arm falls short, the downside could be significant because the stock price already reflects growth expectations that are several years in the future.

data.title: Portillo's: Macroeconomic And Technical Caution Outweigh Valuation
data.description:
In the months that followed my most recent analysis, Portillo’s, Inc. (PTLO) showed some price recovery with 13% returns, which justifies my buy rating before. But today, we may be seeing more headwinds as the macroeconomic environment becomes more volatile amid stubborn inflation and rising oil prices. The market landscape is also becoming increasingly competitive as pricing strategies and promotion from big peers like McDonald’s (MCD) may threaten its sales. The consolation is that its fundamentals still appear strategically positioned to ensure sustainability. Meanwhile, valuation appears to have already priced in new market risks, but technicals are still flat amid consolidation.

Q4 2025: Sales Resilient, But Margin Compressed

The restaurant industry grapples with real recovery as stubborn inflation and tariff woes continue to affect their pricing flexibility and even squeeze their margins. The impact used to be visible only among fast-food restaurants. But today, even many fast-casual restaurants may not be completely shielded as consumer spending softens. Portillo’s, Inc. may not be an exemption, although it continues to demonstrate its resilience in its most recent performance.

In Q4 2025, its operating revenue amounted to $185.7M, up by 0.6% YoY from $184.6M. This was mainly due to its continued expansion through new restaurant openings. In fact, it already had 102 restaurants from 94 YoY. This shows its attempt to increase its domestic market presence and strengthen its brand popularity. However, this could not cover the consequences of unfavorable macroeconomic conditions. Its same-restaurant sales decreased 3.3%. This becomes more evident if we divide the revenue by the number of restaurants. On average, PTLO generated an average revenue of $1.82M ($185.7M / 102 restaurants) per restaurant, which is notably lower than $1.96M ($184.6M /94 restaurants) in the previous year.

Moreover, its operating costs and expenses rose faster than revenues in line with the mounting cost pressures and increased restaurant capacity. On a lighter note, its operating revenue remains large enough to cover all costs and expenses. As a result, its operating margin dropped to 5.6% versus 7.5% YoY but remained positive.

Considerations

As a restaurant, inflation remains a challenge for PTLO. This remains stubborn at 2.4%, although it has already slowed down from 3.0%. After all, it remains higher than the 2.0% target. This may be exacerbated by new tariffs due to their tendency to raise domestic prices. With that, various domestic businesses may raise their prices, which can weaken purchasing power and discretionary spending. Restaurants may experience the negative spillovers.

This may be worsened by the skyrocketing oil prices since they will most likely raise fuel prices. This is important in asset-heavy and large industries like shipping, logistics, industries, and manufacturing. These industries tend to influence macroeconomic fluctuations. So, higher cost pressures in these industries can speed up inflation. PTLO may experience the same thing since it may incur higher delivery costs. It may also have to raise wages, so employees can keep up with the rising prices. Lastly, its ingredients may become more expensive.

Moreover, it must watch out for the stiff market competition. McDonald’s poses another challenge with its cheap pricing and multiple promotions to capture demand and maintain a solid customer base. This move tends to prioritize volume in hopes of offsetting lower prices. And because a typical customer may value cheaper pricing in the current environment, other fast-food and fast-casual restaurants may be affected. Worse, I believe that PTLO cannot do the same to protect its competitiveness. Why? Doing so may compromise its margins. You can see that its sales per restaurant and margins dropped amid rising costs and expenses.

Risk Mitigants

Despite these challenges, I believe that PTLO has some strengths to protect its operations. Before I start, I would like to highlight that these aspects are mere risk mitigants or buffers, not growth drivers.

One aspect to consider is its decent liquidity. Currently, it has cash and cash equivalents of $20M, which is quite enough as a buffer. PTLO complements it with prudent debt management through its strategic and timely issuance and repayments to ensure enough cash inflows. The maturity profile is still decent since over 70% of the total has a maturity of more than one year. To be more specific, $200M will still take five years before its maturity. This is in line with the Net Debt/EBITDA of 3.9x, which means that the company needs 3.9 years theoretically to repay the whole amount even if it doesn’t have to. This means that it remains liquid despite having a moderately high leverage.

The recent policy easing cycle may still have some lingering benefits to cushion the impact of inflation. Lower interest rates may support personal loans and credit card spending on discretionary products and services like restaurants. This may also help PTLO in preserving its liquidity because this may also reduce the repricing rate of variable-rate borrowings.

Valuation

To reassess its valuation, I decided to update price ratios using the same key financial metrics. P/E shows that the stock trades at only 20.38x versus the average of 29.74x. It is not exactly that cheap since it was trading at a much cheaper level in 2024. But it is still much cheaper than in 2022-2023. So, I think it’s trading at a reasonable level. If we use this to derive the fair value, the TP, or target price, will be $7.88. It is also cheaper than MCD and QSR with P/Es of 25.85x and 28.04, respectively.

The same applies to P/S and P/B as PTLO trades cheaper than the four-year average. They suggest TPs of $7.08 and $9.26, respectively. All ratios show that PTLO is still reasonably priced and may have already considered market risks. The challenge right now is how it will warrant upside knowing it lacks growth drivers. It has risk mitigants and robust fundamentals to sustain its operations and cushion the impact of challenges. But it may need to strategize to boost its revenues and earnings to spur its growth.

Technicals

PTLO’s technicals are still flat as the price consolidation continues. From $4.86 in my previous coverage, it is now moving within the $5.50-6.20 range. However, it shows the difficulty of making a bullish breakout. Note that the price highs on February 10, February 24, and March 16 were in a downtrend. Likewise, its price lows were decreasing. Meanwhile, the price is overlapping with the 50-day SMA, which shows that the current momentum appears the same as the average in the past 50 days. This proves that consolidation still holds.

Its MACD and RSI also show the decreasing trend of the price highs and lows. Their consistency shows that there is no bullish divergence yet. Currently, the MACD line is still above the signal line and within positive territory along with the histogram. This implies that buying volume still outpaces selling volume. But you can also see the flattening trend of the MACD line and the decreasing trend of the histogram close to zero, which shows that selling volume is starting to catch up with it. Lastly, RSI appears to be weakening again shortly after the stock regained its momentum. All this shows the cautious stance of the market. It also tells us that profit-takers immediately enter whenever the price rebounds from lows.

Conclusion

Portillo’s, Inc. stays resilient as it stays profitable and liquid amid its continued expansion. However, it lacks growth drivers today due to increased competition and macroeconomic headwinds. The consolation is that valuation has already priced in risks, although not enough to justify more upside. Technicals also signal caution as price consolidation continues. I’m downgrading my rating on this stock from buy to hold.

data.title: Markets Are About To Bounce Higher: 3 Stocks I'm Doubling Down On
data.description:
Introduction

Yesterday, I wrote that the S&P 500 (SPY) is likely setting up for a meaningful bounce (roughly 5%) in the short term after U.S. President Donald Trump posted on Truth Social on Monday, 03/23, where he said that he has ordered a pause on all military strikes against Iranian power plants and energy infrastructure for a five-day period after two days of “very good” conversations with Iran.

Since then, the U.S. has reportedly sent Iran a 15-point plan to end the war in the Middle East, while Iran has also informed the IMO (International Maritime Organization) that vessels deemed “non-hostile” may be allowed to pass through the Strait of Hormuz after the disruption left an estimated 3200 vessels stranded in the Gulf.

Despite the S&P 500 declining 0.37% yesterday, it did not create a lower low, which I believe is constructive. In the meantime, a move over yesterday’s (03/23) high of 6651 will likely trigger the bounce towards the 6900 level that I discussed in my previous post.

Furthermore, what is interesting to note is that the NTM (next twelve months) tech premium relative to the S&P 500 is now completely wiped, based on research from Fundstrat, as can be seen below, which means the sector is the cheapest it has been in a decade.

To further add meat to the above research, Morgan Stanley’s Mike Wilson also published research that points out that the valuation drawdown today is of similar magnitude to what we experienced in the 2015 manufacturing downturn and the 2023 recession scare, despite earnings growth estimates continuing to accelerate, thus lowering the probability of a recession.

In this post, I will discuss three stocks that I have been building a position in over the last couple of months and believe will see strong rallies in the S&P 500 bounce towards 6900.

Meta: The Cleanest AI Story You Can Find Out There

Meta Platforms, Inc. (META) is currently my third largest in the portfolio, and I have plans on expanding it further.

As I explained in the post “Why Meta Is The Best Positioned Hyperscaler In 2026,” I expect Meta’s margins to explode higher as they expand their custom silicon portfolio to bring their inference workloads onto their custom silicon and save billions in annual infrastructure costs. This is the case, as each generation would mark a significant improvement in compute, memory bandwidth, and efficiency, which will help them reduce their dependence on external GPUs and decline over time.

Not only that, but Meta also has one of the strongest AI monetization engines today, which has allowed both revenue and margins to accelerate in their FoA (Family of Apps segment). With its competitive advantage centered on its distribution and having the infrastructure to serve AI at scale, it doesn’t matter whether the company wins in the SOTA AI model race. In fact, if AI models commoditize over time and Meta chooses to abandon its SOTA AI model race, investors will likely cheer the consequent FCF explosion.

On the other hand, if Meta does manage to deliver a SOTA AI model, markets may start pricing a higher premium for being a model platform with proprietary intellectual property at the center of the stack.

Since the time of my writing, the stock has lost 6% of its value, while trading at a non-GAAP forward P/E ratio of 20x, which is the lowest among all hyperscalers. The way I see it, despite the short-term weakness, Meta’s stock price action is still part of a structural bull market, similar to 2022, where I see the stock going at least to $900/share, based on my estimates that I laid out in my previous post, marking a potential upside of 50% from current levels.

The other alternative could be that the stock climbs close to its previous all-time high of $779 per share, in alignment with the 5% or so bounce with the short-term bounce in the S&P 500 that I am expecting, followed by yet another period of volatility inside a structural bull market. In this scenario, we can expect Meta to rise 30% from current levels.

Palantir: The Strongest Software Name Despite “Expensive” Valuation

Since I last wrote about Palantir Technologies Inc. (PLTR) in February and picked it as one of my favorite software names in early March as I expanded my position, the stock is up 14%, outperforming the -4.36% decline in the S&P 500.

While part of the outperformance can be attributed to the improving sentiment in software through March, the other major factor(s) that have also played a role is the growing investor confidence for its AI-driven defense software (Gotham and Foundry) amid intensifying geopolitical conflict in the Middle East and the U.S.

At the same time, Palantir's Maven Smart System received the official program of record designation from the Pentagon on Monday, further solidifying the company's position as a critical provider across the U.S. military.

According to Wedbush analyst Dan Ives,

"Designating Palantir’s Maven Smart System as a program of record will streamline the company's deals across all branches of the military while providing long-term funding to Palantir, with future contracting being handled by the US Army as Maven's oversight will be moved to the Pentagon's Chief Digital AI Office within the next 30 days. Plus, this will allow Palantir to gain more deals across the federal government with its software value proposition playing perfectly into assisting the US government with navigating the complex geopolitical environment, with the company attaching itself to many programs/contracts in the DOD given their high priority while enabling the company to gain more IT budget dollars at the Pentagon.”

As I have written previously, Palantir has been a powerhouse, achieving a Rule of 40 score of 127% in Q4 FY25, which is unmatched by any other enterprise software company, as it managed to expand its profitability with its AIP Bootcamp-led go-to-market strategy, its net retention rate growing for nine consecutive quarters, and revenue momentum showing no signs of slowdown.

Similar to Meta, I believe Palantir stock is also in a structural bull market, despite recent weakness. At the moment, I expect the stock to reach at least $181 per share in the short term, which is my price target that I laid out in my previous moment. This is in alignment with the short-term bounce I am expecting in the S&P 500 index and will represent an upside of 18% from current levels.

MercadoLibre: This Is How an Ecosystem Compounder Looks Like

This brings me to the last company I want to highlight, which is MercadoLibre, Inc. (MELI). After two Hold ratings on the stock, I finally turned bullish back in November. Unfortunately, the stock has been down over 20%+ since then; however, I have used the weakness as an opportunity to build up a position in the stock, which is now my fifth largest holding.

In the company’s Q4 FY25 earnings, revenue accelerated once again, growing 45% YoY, compared to 39% in Q3 and 34% in Q2, led by the company’s largest region, which is Brazil.

In terms of business segments, e-commerce accelerated, driven by a combination of GMV (Gross Merchandise Value) growth and strong performance in advertising revenue. Particularly, when it comes to GMV growth, it was driven by both an increase in buyers on the platform along with an increase in items sold, showcasing real behavior change rather than pricing.

What is also interesting to note is the 74% FX-neutral YoY GMV growth in cross-border trade with the first China fulfillment center now live, as the company invests in the opportunity to expand market share.

Meanwhile, when it comes to its Fintech segment, revenue accelerated as well, growing 51% in Q4, with MAUs (Monthly Active Users) reaching an all-time high of 78M, growing 28% YoY, along with 36% growth in total transactions, credit portfolio doubling to $13B, and 3M cards issued in Q4 alone.

However, the company missed its profitability estimates for the third quarter in a row, with operating margins at 10.1%, which is significantly weaker than what the company used to deliver over the recent years. This is the case, as the company has been heavily investing in free shipping, cross-border trade, and credit penetration across Latin America.

What is also important to note is that earnings per share are expected to increase higher from next quarter onwards per consensus estimates, as shown below.

This is despite the fact that the company has been attracting several downward revisions to its forward earnings per share estimates, while the forward revenue estimates are being revised higher, as can be seen below.

At the moment, the stock is trading at a forward non-GAAP P/E ratio of 31.65, while the average price target on Wall Street stands at $2595 per share, representing an upside of 61% from its current levels.

The way I see it, in alignment with the 5% or so short-term bounce in the S&P 500 that I am expecting, I believe the stock will move higher towards its 200DMA at $2184 per share, which represents an upside of 35% from current levels.

My Final Verdict & Conclusions

At the moment, close to 15% of my portfolio is allocated towards these three names, while I have raised cash to 25.3% of my portfolio size to take advantage of the market volatility in selected names.

I will also point out that given the nature of the market that we are operating in, it is not the time to play "hero." Instead, the way I am navigating this phase of uncertainty and volatility is by surgically increasing my position sizes based on my risk appetite.

At current levels, I might increase my exposure to these three names to 18-20% of the portfolio. Note that even though I believe that these three stocks are in the midst of a structural bull trend despite weakness, I have set aside enough dry powder, especially if the anticipated bounce in the S&P 500 doesn’t work out as planned.

data.title: Abivax: Going It Alone At A Premium (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Overview

To the dismay of many retail investors, Abivax (ABVX) remains a standalone company despite a flurry of rumors. I didn’t mind this much when I last assessed the stock (“Buy”) in January.

You see, Abivax is advancing a first-in-class, oral miR-124 enhancer, obefazimod, in large indications like ulcerative colitis (UC) and Crohn’s disease (CD).

The stock caught everyone’s attention after reporting sparkling Phase 3 UC induction data last Summer.

After jumping over 500% immediately following the news, ABVX has climbed another 60%, as the market has had time to fully digest the results.

I also think some M&A premium is “baked” into the stock.

Abivax reported FY25 results on Monday, bringing into focus its year ahead as a standalone company.

Financials

Abivax ended 2025 with $610M in cash (converted from €530.4M at a 1.15 USD/EUR rate). They believe this will provide a cash runway "into Q4 2027."

For context, Abivax spends roughly $200M and $80M per year in R&D and G&A, respectively. So, its cash runway estimate is pretty consistent with its historical cash burn rate.

Upcoming Milestones

Investors do have a few things to look forward to this year.

UC Phase 3 maintenance results are expected in late Q2. Abivax hopes to then submit a UC NDA in the 2H 2026.

In CD, Phase 2b (ENHANCE-CD) induction results are slated for Q4.

CD

CD induction is a bit of a crowded space, with treatments like Skyrizi and Rinvoq setting a high bar for efficacy.

ENHANCE-CD is a pretty robust trial, so positive results likely de-risk obefazimod’s Phase 3 CD program.

ENHANCE-CD evaluates two separate doses versus placebo for Clinical Remission and Endoscopic Response at Week 12, among a few other outcome measures.

Below, I’ve created a Scenario table that details possible induction outcomes.

Clinical Remission and Endoscopic Response rates >40% would approach high-performing biologics like Skyrizi, which requires intravenous administration at Week 0, Week 4, and Week 8 for the induction phase, followed by subcutaneous injections at Week 12 and every 8 weeks thereafter.

Rinvoq is also orally administered daily and used for induction but isn’t preferred here due to the potential for adverse effects like “serious infections,” “increased risk of death in people 50 years and older who have at least 1 cardiovascular risk factor,” and “blood clots.” There is also a step-through requirement that limits Rinvoq's adoption.

UC

In UC, the Phase 3 maintenance trial (ABTECT) follows the patients who achieved clinical responses in the ABTECT induction phase to evaluate for Clinical Remission at Week 44 of maintenance.

Phase 2b data was published in The Lancet Gastroenterology & Hepatology in 2022.

This trial enrolled 254 patients with moderate-to-severe UC. Roughly half the patients had previously failed biologics or JAK inhibitors.

Results are summarized below.

Now, as is typical in drug development, ABTECT figures to be a tougher test than Phase 2.

For one, ABTECT tightens remission definitions, replacing the more subjective and variable “Physician Global Assessment” with “Modified Mayo Score.”

In this context, anything >50% (Clinical Remission) would be a “home run.” But something in the 40s is probably more realistic.

Pharma DCF

I modeled obefazimod in both UC and CD.

For Target Pop., here is how I see the TAM funnel:

Probability of Success, POS, is higher in UC (75%) than in CD (35%) because the latter is still in Phase 2.

Peak share is higher in UC (20%), given that CD (15%) is more competitive. But both estimates effectively assume obefazimod becomes a standard of care.

I modeled for a Competitive Impact of 15% (impacting market share assumptions) beginning in 2029 to account for share erosion and/or pricing pressure.

I have UC and CD launches slated for 2028 and 2030, respectively. While obefazimod may launch in late 2027 in UC, I like to begin modeling the first full year on the market.

Other assumptions include WACC (12% in CD, 11% in UC), 1.5% annual Pop. Growth, 50% Launch OpEx (for 3 Yrs), 30% Steady-State OpEx, and 8% COGS.

In UC, I estimate that obefazimod is worth $4.35B in risk-adjusted NPV (rNPV) with potential for roughly $3.5B in peak annual sales.

Should maintenance data look good, POS may theoretically bump up to ~90%, resulting in an rNPV of $5.25B.

Obefazimod in CD is worth considerably less ($516.9M in rNPV). This is a result of a few things (i.e., lower POS, 2030 launch, higher WACC).

Should Phase 2 induction data look solid, we may be able to increase POS to ~60% and lower WACC to ~11%.

Doing so would increase obefazimod's CD rNPV to $1.29B.

SOTP

Putting it all together, ABVX looks expensive.

I have its Intrinsic Equity Value at $7.24B. This is the sum of its risk-adjusted pipeline ($4.878B US + $1.95B ROW) and cash ($610M), minus debt (essentially $0) and discounted G&A ($193.8M).

As for cash needs, I did model for a $150M funding gap. This is what is needed to get Abivax to profitability.

While the UC launch is slated for late 2027/early 2028, biopharmas aren’t ordinarily immediately cash-generative. And Abivax will still need to funnel a lot of money into R&D for a Phase 3 CD program. To make up for the $150M funding gap, I assume 0.61% annual dilution at $103.5/share.

In a recent report, the company did signal that they will need to raise additional funds later this year to “take [them] to profitability.”

My $91.59 Fair Value/Share for ABVX is the Post-Raise Equity divided by the Diluted Share Count of 80.7M.

I ordinarily tolerate ~20% variation either way in implied price before questioning results. So, I do think that ABVX is priced quite aggressively.

Scenario Analysis

When my "Base" results are materially different than what’s implied by the market, I like to run a Scenario Analysis.

In a “Bear” scenario, average POS drops <50%, Peak Share <10%, and Net Price <$50k. WACC is boosted to 12.4%.

With these assumptions, ABVX is worth $25.78/share (-77.6% vs. Current Share Price).

In a “Bull” scenario, average POS climbs to 67.5%, Peak Share nears 25%, and Net Price hits $70k.

ABVX is worth $182.16/share in this scenario.

After applying 25/50/25 weights to my “Bear,” “Base,” and “Bull” cases, ABVX’s probability-weighted value comes out to $97.78.

Conclusion

Now that Abivax appears to be going it alone for now (e.g., management is talking about ex-US partners in recent communications), I suspect its share price could fall <$100 ahead of upcoming catalysts (rating downgrade to “Hold”).

Still, I remain very optimistic about the long-term prospects of obefazimod in UC and CD. But I’m in favor of trimming the position ahead of Phase 2 CD and Phase 3 UC data later this year, as expectations are clearly very high.

data.title: American Tower: Buy The Dip
data.description:
It pays to have dry powder on hand, especially when it comes to buy-the-dip opportunities on high-quality stocks. Buying above-average companies at below-average prices can shave years off of one’s wealth compounding journey. Plus, a higher dividend yield due to a lower price can be a great extra boost to total returns.

This brings me to American Tower (AMT), which I last covered back in October 2025, highlighting its robust revenue growth, demand drivers, and strong international presence. AMT has since fallen by 5%, which is in line with the 5% decline in the S&P 500 (SPY) over the same timeframe.

At the current price of $170, AMT trades close to its 52-week low of $167 and carries an appealing 4.2% dividend yield.

In this article, I revisit AMT, including recent business results, and discuss why it’s currently a buy on the recent dip for income and potentially robust total returns, so let’s dive in!

Why AMT?

American Tower is one of the world’s largest owners and operators of wireless communications infrastructure. It has a global portfolio of cell towers and a growing presence in data centers through its CoreSite platform. AMT leases space on its towers to major wireless carriers like AT&T (T), Verizon (VZ), and T-Mobile (TMUS). It also provides interconnection-rich data center services that support cloud, enterprise, and increasingly AI-driven workloads.

In recent years, AMT has seen durable growth from carriers’ 5G network investments and double-digit annual revenue growth in data centers. Its vast scale enables it to standardize and automate its operations, with management expecting 200-300 basis points of adjusted EBITDA margin by the end of this decade.

AMT continued to see robust growth during Q4 2025, with both total revenue and adjusted EBITDA rising by 7.5% YoY to $2.7 billion and $1.8 billion, respectively. AFFO per share grew by an impressive 13.4% and 8% for Q4 and full year 2025, respectively. This was driven by robust leasing demand across both tower and data center segments.

Notably, Africa & APAC were strong drivers of organic tenant billings growth of 8.5%, followed by Europe at 4%. This more than offset a 3% decline in LatAm. As shown below, the U.S. & Canada saw 4.5% growth when excluding the impact from the Dish churn.

Looking ahead, the core drivers of AMT’s growth remain intact as they are tied to global data consumption trends. Mobile data demand continues to expand at a rapid pace due to increasing smartphone penetration around the globe, 5G adoption, and fixed wireless access for home broadband. This is not to mention emerging AI applications that will drive the next phase of demand.

This includes video-driven AI, which will drive increasing workloads across connectivity, as noted by management during the recent earnings call:

And when it comes to AI, we're in the early days of this. It's really video that puts the stress on the networks, and it's both video upstreaming and down-streaming. And that's what we think is going to drive a lot more activity over time.

Most networks today have about 10% dedicated to upload and 90% to download. We think that in the future AI could change that trajectory a bit so that you're seeing north of 20% in terms of upload capacity.

Too hard to predict exactly when it's going to happen or exactly what app is going to drive it. But it's really that video upstreaming, video manipulation as well as things like the Meta glasses that are live-streaming kind of everything around you. Those types of applications that we think are going to really result in some network traffic over time.

Importantly, AMT carries a strong balance sheet with a BBB+ credit rating from S&P. This is supported by a net debt-to-EBITDA ratio of 4.9x, which improved from 5.1x at the end of 2024. AMT also has $11 billion of liquidity, and 96% of its debt is held at a floating rate.

The lower share price combined with a dividend increase this year has driven the yield up to 4.2%. The dividend is well covered by a 73% payout ratio. AMT has raised its dividend for 13 years straight and raised it by 5.3% this year. The dividend track record includes a dividend rate reduction in 2024, with the full year’s dividend still being higher than that of 2023.

Lastly, I see solid value in AMT at the current price of $170 with a forward P/FFO of 17.4, sitting well under its historical P/FFO of 22.2, as shown below.

At the current valuation, the market isn’t giving AMT enough credit for its moat-worthy asset base with scale and improving cost efficiency. In addition, AMT has solid growth drivers stemming from the proliferation of AI, which will undoubtedly increase network utilization. With a 4.2% yield, my expectations for 6-10% annual FFO/share growth, and a reversion to the mean valuation, AMT could produce double-digit total returns from here.

Risks to AMT include the concentration of its tenant base around the major wireless carriers. Churn due to industry consolidation could result in a pullback in network investments by carriers. Satellite technology could also represent competition, but thus far this technology has been complementary to terrestrial networks, and that should be the case for the foreseeable future.

Investor Takeaway

American Tower is a high-quality infrastructure REIT trading near its 52-week low, offering a compelling mix of income and growth. Its diversified geographic footprint and expanding data center platform support business resiliency. AMT is well-positioned to deliver steady revenue and FFO growth, driven by global data consumption, 5G expansion, and emerging AI-driven network demand.

Lastly, AMT has a solid balance sheet and long dividend track record supporting its 4.2% yield. The historically high yield and discounted valuation could result in potentially meaningful total returns for investors from here. For these reasons, I maintain a ‘Strong Buy’ on AMT on the dip.
